text.title: Architecture Workflow Types
text.summary: Seven repeatable architecture workflow types — system design, ADR, technology evaluation, ATAM/CBAM, migration planning, design-document review, and architecture review — each as a named phase pipeline with step sequences, key outputs, and agent-decomposition guidance.

rule.statement: Always decompose into exactly six phases: CLARIFY → ESTIMATE → DESIGN → DEEP-DIVE → TRADE-OFFS → DOCUMENT. Never merge TRADE-OFFS into DESIGN — the validation step requires a separate adversarial pass.
rule.rationale: Merged design+validation phases produce rubber-stamp output; the same persona that designed cannot adversarially attack it.

list.item: CLARIFY: extract functional requirements, non-functional requirements (with numeric targets), constraints, and explicit non-goals. Output: scoped spec.md.
ESTIMATE: back-of-envelope — DAU → RPS → storage → bandwidth. Output: scale-estimates.md with at least four numeric rows.
DESIGN: produce three architecture options with explicit NFR scores. Single-option output is a failure signature. Output: options.md with trade-off table.
DEEP-DIVE: detail the chosen option — data model, API contracts, critical paths (caching, queuing, DB schema). Output: design.md.
TRADE-OFFS: separate adversarial call — SPOFs, hot partitions, data-loss windows, security surface. Output: risks.md.
DOCUMENT: generate C4 L1+L2 diagrams (Mermaid), one ADR per non-obvious decision. Output: diagrams/ + decisions/.

rule.statement: Treat ADRs like code: versioned, reviewed via PR, lifecycle-tracked (proposed → accepted → deprecated → superseded). One ADR per decision.
rule.rationale: ADRs stored in prose wikis drift and become orphaned; Git history gives authorship, review comments, and diff trails.

list.item: IDENTIFY: flag the decision — significant, non-obvious, or with multiple viable alternatives. Trivial decisions are not worth an ADR.
DRAFT: fill MADR template — context (1–2 sentences), decision (one sentence + primary reason), alternatives table (pros/cons), consequences (positive + negative).
REVIEW: async PR review or a timed readout (10–15 min reading + 30 min discussion). Push for decision in 1–3 readouts; open-ended ADRs stall.
DECIDE: change status to "Accepted" or "Rejected." Rejected ADRs are preserved — they explain why a path was not taken.
MAINTAIN: after-action review at 30 and 90 days. Feed findings into the next evaluation round. Supersede rather than delete when the decision changes.

rule.statement: Force the evaluator agent to cite at least one source per claim (release notes, benchmarks, official docs). Uncited claims are hallucination candidates.
rule.rationale: LLM-generated technology comparisons frequently invent performance numbers; citations make claims falsifiable.

list.item: DEFINE CRITERIA: list weighted criteria from NFRs — latency, throughput, operational complexity, licensing cost, team expertise, ecosystem health.
RESEARCH: gather evidence per option. Use WebSearch for release notes, benchmarks, CVE history. Minimum one cited source per claim.
EVALUATE: build a weighted-score matrix. Require score differentiation — reject all-8/10 outputs.
DECIDE: recommendation must survive steel-manning the runner-up — explicitly address the runner-up's strongest argument.
DOCUMENT: write ADR with the matrix, citations, and PoC result if one was run.

section.title: ATAM Assessment Workflow

rule.statement: ATAM utility trees from LLMs are usually too uniform — every leaf marked "high importance, high difficulty." Require an explicit ranking distribution (no more than 20% of leaves at the highest tier).
rule.rationale: Uniform trees provide no prioritization signal; the point of ATAM is to surface the top-3 risk themes, not to enumerate everything.

list.item: Step 1: Present business drivers — goals, constraints, key stakeholders.
Step 2: Present architecture — C4 L1+L2, key decisions.
Step 3: Identify architectural approaches — patterns used (caching, queuing, sharding).
Step 4: Generate quality-attribute utility tree — root QA branches (availability, performance, security, modifiability) with scenarios at leaves.
Step 5: Analyze approaches against utility tree — identify sensitivity points and trade-off points.
Step 6: Brainstorm and prioritize scenarios — stakeholder input, rank by H/M/L importance × difficulty.
Step 7: Refined analysis — address top scenarios against the architecture.
Step 8: Present results — sensitivity points, trade-off points, risk themes (top 3).

section.note: CBAM extends ATAM with cost/benefit: estimate cost for each sensitivity point, quantify benefit of each quality-attribute improvement, compute ROI. Apply only when budget decisions are at stake.

rule.statement: Require the agent to draft the rollback plan before the cutover plan. Migration plans without rollback are incomplete.
rule.rationale: Agents skip rollback, dual-write, and feature-flag cutover unless they are required headings in the template.

list.item: IDENTIFY: select the component for migration — highest risk, highest coupling, or highest business value first.
ISOLATE: implement the replacement in the new platform with full feature parity and tests. Do not touch the legacy path yet.
ROUTE: use API gateway or feature flag to redirect a percentage of traffic to the new implementation. Start at 1%.
DUAL-WRITE (if stateful): write to both old and new stores; verify consistency before cutting read traffic.
TEST: canary at 1% → 10% → 50% → 100%. Gate each step on error rate and p95 latency parity.
CUTOVER: switch 100%, monitor for 24h, then decommission the legacy path. Rollback plan must be documented and tested before cutover.
ITERATE: repeat for the next component. Migration is complete only when the legacy system is decommissioned.

section.title: Design Document Review Workflow

list.item: IDENTIFY: in sprint planning, flag architecturally significant work (new service, schema change, external API contract).
PREPARE: author circulates design.md 48h before the review meeting. Reviewers read async.
REVIEW: timed meeting — 30 min max. Focus on requirements coverage, NFR targets, trade-offs, and open questions.
FEEDBACK: written, actionable comments in the PR. No verbal-only feedback — it does not persist.
APPROVE: author addresses comments, updates design.md, merges. ADRs are merged alongside code in the same PR.

section.note: CI gate: if architecture-impacting paths change (infra/, deploy/, services/*/api/, docs/architecture/) without an ADR update, fail the PR. See templates for the arch-review-check.sh script.
